content:
Some preliminary comments from me:

- This is a good introductory problem to dynamic programming and it is quite unique in terms of having the constraint being "exactly equal" instead of "at most", which limits the problem somewhat.

- Currently, the constraints specified are not reflected accurately in the test cases. The maximum capacity (which are all currently <50) is way too small to require comparing multiple subsets of items against one another, and the whole puzzle is currently brute-forceable. As it stands, the puzzle does not warrant the "Hard" difficulty. Please generate larger test cases.

- In terms of formatting, I would format -1 as a constant. The last sentence of the goal could also be stated clearer; "If it is not possible for the spaceship to carry items weighing exactly [[capacity]], output {{-1}} instead."

Hi everyone,
I’ve just published this puzzle as a Work In Progress and would really appreciate your feedback.
The goal was to create a simple but non-trivial optimization problem around an exact-weight knapsack variant, while keeping the statement clear and accessible.
I’m especially interested in your thoughts on:
- the clarity of the statement
- the difficulty level (currently set to “Hard”)
- the quality and progression of the test cases
- any potential edge cases I might have missed
If something feels unclear, too easy, too hard, or just “off”, feel free to point it out.
Thanks a lot for your time and help !

Thanks a lot for your detailed feedback.
I've updated the puzzle accordingly:

- The larger test cases have been reworked with significantly higher capacities and more items, so brute-force approaches are no longer viable and the difficulty better matches the intended "Hard" level.

- I also adjusted the constraints to better reflect the actual datasets used in the tests.

- The statement has been clarified, and {{-1}} is now formatted as a constant as suggested.

While working on the larger cases, I also improved the reference solution to ensure it handles them efficiently.

Thanks again for the helpful remarks!
